channel.title: NPR Topics: Education
channel.description: We've been to school. We know how education works. Right? In fact, many aspects of learning — in homes, at schools, at work and elsewhere — are evolving rapidly, along with our understanding of learning. Join us as we explore how learning happens.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Kids' test scores began declining way before COVID. These schools are making gains
item.description: The annual Education Scorecard shows the nation's schools still rebounding from serious losses in math and reading, but it also found those declines began well before the pandemic.
item.content:encoded: The annual Education Scorecard shows the nation's schools still rebounding from serious losses in math and reading, but it also found those declines began well before the pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Cory Turner

item.title: Kids' test scores began declining way before COVID. These schools are making gains
item.description: Remember those devastating learning losses that began during the pandemic? Turns out, they began years before COVID-19. Some states are finally turning things around.
item.content:encoded: Remember those devastating learning losses that began during the pandemic? Turns out, they began years before COVID-19. Some states are finally turning things around.
item.dc:creator: Cory Turner

item.title: Oregon's most unexpected gubernatorial candidate? A pencil with a point
item.description: Oregon's public schools rank last in fourth-grade reading, according to an analysis of national testing. As a wake-up call for elected leaders, Pencil is running for governor as a write-in candidate.
item.content:encoded:
Oregon's public schools rank last in fourth-grade reading, according to an analysis of national testing. As a wake-up call for elected leaders, Pencil is running for governor as a write-in candidate.

(Image credit: Eli Imadali)
item.dc:creator: Dirk VanderHart

item.title: Canvas is back online, but questions — and final exam disruptions — linger
item.description: Some schools are warning users not to log back into Canvas yet, after a ransomware group claimed credit for a data breach. Half of North America's higher education institutions use the platform.
item.content:encoded:
Some schools are warning users not to log back into Canvas yet, after a ransomware group claimed credit for a data breach. Half of North America's higher education institutions use the platform.

(Image credit: Michael Warren)
item.dc:creator: Rachel Treisman

item.title: Making a podcast helped one family talk about aging, dementia and death
item.description: This year's winner in NPR's College Podcast Challenge is a letter to a grandparent that grapples with health issues including dementia. It's the story of a family learning to talk about hard things.
item.content:encoded:
This year's winner in NPR's College Podcast Challenge is a letter to a grandparent that grapples with health issues including dementia. It's the story of a family learning to talk about hard things.

(Image credit: Matthew Coughlin for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Elissa Nadworny

item.title: New study measures whether school cell phone bans actually work
item.description: A first-of-its-kind national study looks at the impacts of cell phone bans in schools.
item.content:encoded: A first-of-its-kind national study looks at the impacts of cell phone bans in schools.
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Carrillo

item.title: The Trump administration is investigating Smith College for admitting trans women
item.description: Smith College is one of the largest women's colleges in the country. This week, the Trump administration announced it is investigating the school's practice of admitting trans women.
item.content:encoded: Smith College is one of the largest women's colleges in the country. This week, the Trump administration announced it is investigating the school's practice of admitting trans women.
item.dc:creator: Nirvani Williams

item.title: More college applicants are opting to include SAT or ACT scores in their submissions
item.description: This application cycle, way more students reported test scores, up 10% compared with last year. That's despite many colleges having "test-optional" admissions policies.
item.content:encoded: This application cycle, way more students reported test scores, up 10% compared with last year. That's despite many colleges having "test-optional" admissions policies.
item.dc:creator: JD Allen

item.title: What actually happens when schools take away students' phones?
item.description: NPR's Ailsa Chang speaks with Stanford education economist Thomas Dee about the latest study on schools banning cellphones.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ailsa Chang speaks with Stanford education economist Thomas Dee about the latest study on schools banning cellphones.
item.dc:creator: Brianna Scott

item.title: A new association of 15 HBCUs aims to collectively boost their research status
item.description: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to Morgan State University president David K. Wilson about an association aimed at boosting the research status of Historically Black Colleges and Universities.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to Morgan State University president David K. Wilson about an association aimed at boosting the research status of Historically Black Colleges and Universities.
item.dc:creator: Ayesha Rascoe
